text: The surgeon positions Maryland bipolar forceps in the left robotic arm, monopolar scissors in the right arm, and tenaculum forceps in the third arm, preparing for precise manipulation during the prostatectomy.
The sigmoid colon is visible below, with the outline of the bladder seen ahead in the surgical field.
The surgeon makes an incision lateral to the left medial umbilical ligament, using robotic-assisted instruments visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon makes an incision lateral to the right medial umbilical ligament, mirroring the incision previously made on the left side.
After completing the incisions, the surgeon transects the medial umbilical ligaments and the urachus using robotic instruments, with the patient positioned in a steep Trendelenburg position.
The surgeon develops the space of Retzius using a combination of blunt dissection and cautery, manipulating tissues to create a clear surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the pubic bone, with the bladder and prostate visible in the surgical field.
With the space of Retzius developed, the surgeon manipulates the Foley catheter to visually identify the bladder neck, which is positioned anteriorly to the prostate.
The surgeon opens the bladder transversely, making an incision approximately three centimeters above the bladder neck, revealing the internal bladder structures.
The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to continue dissection until the Foley catheter balloon becomes visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses tenaculum forceps to elevate the anterior lip of the bladder wall, providing retraction and improved visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to open the bladder wall, extending the incision laterally until reaching the visible lateral limits of the prostate.
Bladder stones are visually identified and removed using a specimen retrieval bag inserted through the eleven millimeter assistant port.
The surgeon removes five bladder stones using a specimen retrieval bag through the assistant port, with the stones clearly visible as they are extracted from the bladder.
The surgeon uses a tenaculum to identify the posterior bladder neck, providing a clear view of the anatomical structure during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the dissection by entering the plane between the prostate adenoma and the prostatic capsule, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon uses a combination of bipolar and monopolar energy to cauterize tissue and control bleeding within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection and cautery to extend the circumferential plane of dissection around the prostate adenoma, separating it from the surrounding prostatic capsule.
The surgeon uses the tenaculum to retract tissue, providing better visualization and access to the surgical field.
The prostatic adenoma is visibly separating from the prostatic capsule, revealing a clear plane between the two structures.
The surgeon moves the dissection anteriorly after reaching the posterior and lateral limits, continuing to separate the prostate adenoma from the prostatic capsule.
The surgeon places a zero bicoral state suture on a CT needle into the anterior bladder wall, serving as a holding stitch to maintain tissue position during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a fascial closure device to bring the suture through the anterior abdominal wall, securing the tissue in place.
The surgeon continues to dissect anteriorly around the prostate, carefully separating the tissue from the surrounding structures.
The prostatic adenoma is visibly almost completely separated from the surrounding capsule, indicating the nearing completion of its dissection.
The adenoma, now entirely free from surrounding tissue, is placed into a specimen retrieval bag for removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the prostate specimen in two pieces, likely using a specimen retrieval bag as previously prepared.
The surgeon uses microsutures to control the pedicles located at the five and seven o'clock positions around the prostate, ensuring precise management of these structures.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 microsuture to approximate the urethra to the trigone of the bladder, ensuring precise alignment of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon secures the adenoma using a surgical slip knot, completing its detachment for removal.
The surgeon places a total of five interrupted 2-0 microsutures in the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the urethral stents, which were previously placed during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon inserts a 22 French three-way Foley catheter into the urethra.
The surgeon closes the bladder using two layers of running 2-0 Vicryl sutures, ensuring secure closure of the bladder wall.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of the first layer of sutures, likely involving the closure of the bladder using running sutures.
The surgeon fills the bladder with 200 cc of normal saline, visibly distending it.
The surgical field shows no visible evidence of fluid leakage from the closed bladder.
The surgeon completes the closure of the bladder by finishing the second layer with running sutures, ensuring the bladder is securely sealed.
The surgeon places a closed suction device in the surgical field, ensuring proper positioning for postoperative drainage.
Under laparoscopic visualization, the surgeon removes all trocars from the abdominal cavity, ensuring clear observation of the surgical field during the removal process.
The assistant port is visually inspected and closed if necessary, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon removes the specimen and proceeds to close the skin and fascia layers.
